system_description.overview: Alma is a technology platform enabling provision of immigration legal services. It combines advanced AI with software engineering to automate and streamline immigration workflows including case management, compliance, reporting/analytics, and billing. It empowers legal teams and their clients to navigate complex immigration processes.

products.Immigration Case & Compliance Management: End-to-end automation for employment-based immigration workflows including case initiation, document intake, petition drafting, and compliance tracking across visa categories (H-1B, O-1, L-1, EB-1). Integrates with client HRIS, ATS, and document management systems.
products.AI-Powered Immigration Risk & Process Co: Automated document scanning to detect missing or non-compliant materials, continuous monitoring of case data and timelines, role-based access controls, automated responses to immigration questionnaires.
products.Implementation & Onboarding Services: Client-specific platform setup, process mapping, and workflow configuration.
products.Ongoing Immigration Program Support: Access to immigration attorneys and legal specialists, proactive case monitoring, coordination with petition reviewers and government liaisons.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out — AWS controls excluded from scope)
infrastructure.physical_presence: No physical offices or servers — fully cloud-hosted. HQ: 545 Bryant Street, Palo Alto, CA (workspace managed by building management)
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (us-west-2a and us-west-2b confirmed in network diagram)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Segmented VPC (alma-vpc) with public subnets (alma-subnet-public1/2) and private subnets (alma-subnet-private1/2). ACLs in use. Security groups wrapping ECS clusters in private subnets.
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS employed via AWS to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings.
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via bastion host with VPN. Bastion host sits in public subnet with VPN.
network_architecture.tls: TLS used for all web-based requests; certificates from an established third-party certificate authority. Encryption technologies protect communication over public networks.
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server; internet access restricted for all systems.
network_architecture.topology: Internet -> alma-igw (Internet Gateway) -> alma-vpc -> public subnets (us-west-2a/2b) with NAT Gateways and Application Load Balancers -> private subnets (us-west-2a/2b) with ECS Cluster Containers inside Security Groups.

application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test environment from production (changes tested before implementation). Stage and Production environments mentioned.

databases: Not explicitly named — cloud database mentioned as part of infrastructure
file_storage: Not explicitly named — cloud storage implied
data_storage.backup_frequency: Backup policy in place; specific frequency not disclosed beyond 'suitable backups taken and maintained'

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: AWS (Active Directory mentioned for production environment and infrastructure accounts; passwords controlled through AWS password policy)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform. MFA required to access source code management tool. MFA required for employee email access.
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination of employment
authentication_access_control.password_policy: AWS-managed password policy including periodic forced changes, password expiry, complexity requirements, and account lockout after limited failed logon attempts. Guest/anonymous logins prohibited. Unattended desktops locked on inactivity.

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest
encryption.key_management: Cloud provider KMS (AWS KMS implied); access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool (vendor not named — GitHub/GitLab implied by branch protection rules and MFA access requirement)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured so every merge request to production requires additional approval. Developers cannot make changes to production code without additional approval.
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configurations with rollback capability (vendor not named)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO-managed automatic patch management with monthly reviews. Workstations auto-update and scanned for patch compliance daily. Production servers patched on critical/security basis.

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named — internal monitoring tool generating alerts on predefined thresholds
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled at application and infrastructure level monitoring admin activities, logon attempts, data deletions, function changes, security configurations, permissions, and roles. Log access restricted.
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events via monitoring tool; resolved through incident management process.

third_party_services.purpose: Cloud infrastructure, data center services, VPC, ECS, load balancing, KMS, IDS/IPS

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed on all production servers (continuous auto-scan; definitions updated weekly on servers, daily on workstations). Installed on Mac workstations and laptops. Endpoint Security Solution with automatic scanning.
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications. Self-assessment scan prior to every production release. Internal IT Infrastructure team conducts periodic vulnerability scans per audit calendar.
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party. High/critical findings remediated in timely manner and tracked via incident management.

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access to data center facilities: AWS responsible for physical security of data center; controls not in scope
A1.2 — Environmental protections: Alma has no office/physical servers; managed by building management/AWS
Processing Integrity (PI) — Alma determined not relevant to system
Privacy — Alma determined not relevant to system
notable_controls: CC8.1.2: Code repository branch rules require additional approval on every merge request to production — direct evidence of enforced branch protection
CC6.2.2: Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged users
CC6.1.4: Access revoked within one business day of termination
CC4.1.1: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party
CC7.1.2: Monthly automated vulnerability scans with remediation tracking
CC6.6.1: Firewalls and intrusion detection tools deployed; firewall rules reviewed annually
CC7.4.1: BCDR policy documented and tested annually across multiple loss scenarios
CC9.2.1: Annual SOC report reviews for vendors; quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports
CC5.2.1: Continuous capacity/performance monitoring with automated threshold alerting

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-AZ AWS deployment in us-west-2 with VPC, public/private subnet segmentation, NAT Gateways, ALBs, and ECS containers. Solid architecture. Single region only (no multi-region failover) and no CDN mentioned. Configuration management tool with IaC-like baseline management, but specific tool unnamed.

application_architecture.rationale: ECS containers suggest microservices orientation. Branch protection and code review enforced. Separate dev/test/staging/production environments. However no languages, frameworks, or specific CI/CD tooling named. AI/ML team exists but tech stack undisclosed.

data_layer.rationale: Data classification policy and retention policy exist. Per-tenant data segregation confirmed. Multi-AZ hosting. However, no specific database technology named, no caching layer mentioned, no data warehouse disclosed, backup frequency unspecified beyond 'suitable backups maintained'.

security_posture.rationale: Strong: annual external pen testing, monthly automated vuln scans, IDS/IPS, firewalls, MFA on admin accounts and source code access, quarterly access reviews, antivirus with continuous scanning, server hardening with configuration baselines, log protection. Gaps: no WAF named, no SIEM named, no cybersecurity insurance disclosed.

devops_maturity.rationale: ECS container orchestration, branch protection enforced via repo rules, separate environments, emergency change process, monthly patch reviews by CTO. Config management tool in use. Source code tool requires MFA. Gaps: CI/CD tool unnamed, IaC tool unnamed, no mention of automated testing pipelines.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing across multiple loss scenarios with results tracked and plans updated. Multi-AZ failover by spinning up servers across AZs. Incident response plan integrated. Gaps: no multi-region failover, no RTO/RPO disclosed, backup frequency unspecified.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Only one third-party vendor explicitly named: AWS. All infrastructure, compute, networking, KMS, and IDS/IPS runs on AWS. No secondary cloud, CDN, monitoring tool, identity provider, or other SaaS vendors named in the report. Extreme single-vendor concentration.

overall.rationale: Alma shows a solid foundational security posture for an early-stage company — multi-AZ AWS architecture with proper network segmentation, MFA, quarterly access reviews, annual pen testing, and monthly vuln scans are genuine strengths. Primary concerns are extreme AWS concentration (single named vendor), no disclosed RTO/RPO, no named CI/CD or monitoring toolchain, and thin data layer disclosure. This is a first SOC 2 Type 1 (as of July 2025) so maturity is expected to be lower than Type 2 peers.

red_flags: Extreme single-vendor concentration: AWS is the only subservice organization named; no secondary cloud, CDN, SIEM, APM, or other tooling vendors disclosed anywhere in report
No RTO or RPO disclosed — BCDR plan exists and is tested annually but time-based recovery targets are absent
No WAF mentioned despite web-based SaaS product handling sensitive immigration records
Backup frequency and data storage technology entirely undisclosed — cannot assess data layer risk
yellow_flags: No multi-region failover — AZ-level resilience only within us-west-2; regional AWS outage would affect availability
CI/CD tooling, IaC tool, and source code management platform all unnamed — cannot assess vendor risk or maturity of pipeline
No SIEM named — logging infrastructure described but no aggregation/analysis platform identified
SOC 2 Type 1 only (as of July 2025) — design-only assessment; no operating effectiveness tested yet
Processing Integrity and Privacy criteria excluded from scope — notable for a platform handling sensitive immigration case data
No specific database technology named — PostgreSQL, RDS, DynamoDB etc. all unknown
green_flags: Multi-AZ deployment with documented network diagram showing VPC, public/private subnets, ALBs, NAT Gateways, and ECS containers across us-west-2a and us-west-2b
Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party with tracked remediation
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning on infrastructure and applications
MFA enforced on admin access to production platform AND source code management tool
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
Access revocation SLA of one business day — explicitly enforced via termination checklist
Branch protection enforced via repo rules — every merge to production requires additional approval
BCDR tested annually across multiple loss scenarios with results driving plan updates
Quarterly vendor risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports
Server hardening with configuration baselines retained as code with rollback capability
Antivirus continuous scanning on production servers with weekly definition updates
Content filtering via proxy server for all systems
Unqualified opinion with zero exceptions — clean first SOC 2
key_observations: Alma is a very early-stage compliance program — this is their first SOC 2 (Type 1, July 2025). The clean unqualified opinion is a positive signal but Type 1 only attests to design, not operational effectiveness over time.

The network diagram on page 18 is a genuine differentiator in this report — it explicitly shows ECS Cluster Containers in private subnets behind ALBs with NAT Gateways, confirming a containerized microservices architecture. This is more architectural transparency than most Accorp-prepared reports provide.

AI/ML team is listed in the org chart (under CTO alongside Back-end and Front-end Engineering) but zero AI infrastructure (model hosting, vector databases, LLM API providers) is disclosed anywhere in the report — given the product's AI-driven core, this is a material gap.

The report explicitly excludes CC6.4 (physical access) and A1.2 (environmental protections) with the justification that Alma has no physical office and all infrastructure is on AWS — confirming fully cloud-native with no co-location.

Password policy is described as 'controlled through Password policy of AWS' — suggesting IAM-native password management rather than a dedicated IdP like Okta or Active Directory. This is unusual for a B2B SaaS and may indicate early-stage identity infrastructure.

Only one subservice organization (AWS) is disclosed and carved out. The absence of any other named vendors (no Stripe, no Salesforce, no Intercom, no OpenAI, no Twilio) is atypical — either the vendor stack is unusually minimal or vendor disclosures were kept narrow for the report scope.

The immigration domain involves highly sensitive PII (visa applications, passport data, work authorization records) but Privacy trust criteria is explicitly excluded from scope. This may become a customer concern as the company scales enterprise sales.
